intro.overview: This week you'll complete NEXUS reconnaissance training by exploring six essential security tools. Each tool serves a specific purpose in the information-gathering phase of security assessments. You'll practice on authorized targets and document your methodology — next week, you'll apply these skills against a real client engagement.

mentorQuote.title: Senior Penetration Tester, NEXUS Security
mentorQuote.quote: Reconnaissance is where every engagement begins. Before you touch a target, you need to understand what you're dealing with. These six tools are your foundation. Master them here on authorized targets so you're confident when you face a real client network next week. Document everything — your runbook should be detailed enough that another analyst could reproduce your methodology.

focusAreas: Network scanning and service enumeration with nmap
Vulnerability research using searchsploit and Exploit-DB
objectives: Demonstrate network scanning with nmap (version and script scans)
Conduct web directory discovery with dirb
Execute web vulnerability scanning with nikto
Explore SMB enumeration capabilities with enum4linux

callouts.title: Save For Lab 1.3
callouts.text: This is Week 2 of a 3-week module. Continue saving screenshots to your Module1_Screenshots folder. You'll compile these into your Lab 1.3 Engagement Packet next week.

rows.task: Read instructions and review authorized targets

timeGuide.timesheetNote: This is Week 2 of 3. Log these hours as Internal Training on your Lab 1.3 timesheet. Continue saving screenshots to your Module1_Screenshots folder — screenshots 07–13 go directly into your Engagement Packet next week.

content.whatYoureDoing: Demonstrating network scanning fundamentals using nmap against authorized targets. You'll perform version detection and script scanning to identify services and potential vulnerabilities.
content.whyItMatters: Nmap is the industry-standard network scanner. Understanding its capabilities helps both attackers (who use it to map target networks) and defenders (who need to understand what attackers see). Version detection reveals exactly what software is running, which directly informs vulnerability research.

steps.text: Open a terminal in your Kali VM
Run a version detection scan:

whatThisDoes.concept: The -sV flag tells Nmap to probe open ports and determine what service and version is running on each one. Without it, Nmap only tells you a port is open — with it, you learn what software is listening (e.g., Apache 2.4.7, OpenSSH 6.6.1p1).
whatThisDoes.output: Look for the VERSION column in the results table. Each open port should show specific software and version numbers. This information is what makes vulnerability research possible.

steps.text: Note the open ports and service versions detected
Run a default script scan:

whatThisDoes.concept: The -sC flag runs Nmap’s default set of scripts against discovered services. These scripts perform safe enumeration tasks like grabbing SSH host keys, checking HTTP headers, and detecting common misconfigurations.
whatThisDoes.output: Compare this to the -sV output. You should see additional details like SSH host keys and HTTP page titles that weren’t in the version scan. Scripts extend Nmap from a port scanner into an information-gathering platform.

steps.text: Compare the output — what additional information did scripts reveal?

content.title: Key Flags
items: -sV — Version detection
-sC — Default scripts
-A — Aggressive (combines -sV, -sC, OS detection, traceroute)

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture nmap -sV results. Save as 07-nmap-version.png

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture nmap -sC results. Save as 08-nmap-scripts.png

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Scan takes forever: Use -T4 for faster timing
Permission denied: Use sudo if needed
No results: Test connectivity with ping scanme.nmap.org

content.title: What You Should Have
content.text: Scan results showing open ports (typically 22/SSH, 80/HTTP) with service version information like OpenSSH 6.6.1p1 or Apache httpd 2.4.7.

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 2 discusses threat intelligence gathering and reconnaissance techniques. Port scanning is a fundamental reconnaissance activity that provides the foundation for vulnerability assessment.

content.title: Dig Deeper
content.text: What’s the difference between a TCP connect scan (-sT) and a SYN scan (-sS)? Why might an attacker prefer one over the other?

content.title: That’s a Reference!
content.text: Nmap Reference Guide. Gordon Lyon. Available at: https://nmap.org/book/man.html

content.whatYoureDoing: Using netcat to manually connect to network services and capture their identification banners — the text services display when you connect.
content.whyItMatters: Service banners reveal software versions, hostnames, and configuration details. This low-level technique helps verify nmap results and often reveals information automated scanners miss. Netcat is the “Swiss Army knife” of networking.

steps.text: Connect to SSH port on scanme.nmap.org:

whatThisDoes.concept: nc (netcat) opens a raw TCP connection to a host and port. The -v flag enables verbose output so you can see the connection status. Port 22 is the standard SSH port.
whatThisDoes.output: You should see a connection message followed by the SSH banner (something like SSH-2.0-OpenSSH_6.6.1p1). This banner reveals the exact software version — valuable intel for an attacker.

steps.text: Observe the banner that appears (SSH version string)
Press Ctrl+C to disconnect
Try connecting to port 80:

whatThisDoes.concept: Port 80 is the standard HTTP port. Unlike SSH, web servers don’t send a banner automatically — you need to send an HTTP request first. This teaches you that different protocols behave differently during reconnaissance.
whatThisDoes.output: You’ll see a connection confirmation, but no banner yet. You need to type the HTTP request in the next step to get a response.

steps.text: Type HEAD / HTTP/1.0 and press Enter twice to get HTTP headers

whatThisDoes.concept: HEAD / HTTP/1.0 is a raw HTTP request asking the server for headers only (no page content). The response reveals the web server software, version, and sometimes OS details. This is manual banner grabbing for HTTP — exactly what automated tools do behind the scenes.

content.title: Pro Tip
content.text: On Kali Linux, nc may be aliased to ncat. Both work the same for banner grabbing.

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture netcat connection showing SSH banner. Save as 09-netcat-banner.png

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Connection refused: Port may be filtered or service down — try a different port
Timeout: Add -w 5 for 5-second timeout
No banner: Some services don’t display banners until you send data

content.title: What You Should Have
content.text: Screenshot showing connection and banner text like SSH-2.0-OpenSSH_...

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 2 discusses information disclosure as a security concern. Banner grabbing demonstrates how services can inadvertently reveal version information that aids attackers.

content.title: Dig Deeper
content.text: How can administrators hide or modify service banners? Research banner hardening techniques for SSH and HTTP.

content.title: That’s a Reference!
content.text: Ncat Users’ Guide. Nmap Project. Available at: https://nmap.org/ncat/guide/

content.whatYoureDoing: Using dirb to brute-force discover hidden directories and files on web servers — finding content that isn’t linked from the main site.
content.whyItMatters: Web apps often have hidden admin panels, backup files, or development directories. Directory brute-forcing finds these resources that attackers commonly exploit.

steps.text: Run dirb against the test target:

whatThisDoes.concept: dirb sends HTTP requests for thousands of common directory and file names (from a wordlist) and reports which ones exist. It’s a brute-force approach to content discovery — finding pages the site doesn’t link to publicly.
whatThisDoes.output: Watch for lines showing CODE:200 (the page exists) versus CODE:403 (forbidden but exists) and CODE:301 (redirect). Each discovered path is a potential attack surface.

steps.text: Watch it test common directory names
Note which return CODE:200 (found)
Let it run or press Ctrl+C after several results

content.title: Wordlist Location
content.text: Default wordlists: /usr/share/dirb/wordlists/

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture dirb output showing discovered directories. Save as 10-dirb-results.png

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Forgetting http://: URL must include the protocol — dirb http://target not just dirb target
Slow scan: Dirb tests thousands of paths — use Ctrl+C after seeing results for training purposes
Connection errors: Target site must be reachable — test with curl first if dirb fails

content.title: What You Should Have
content.text: Output showing discovered directories like /admin/, /images/, /cgi-bin/ with HTTP response codes.

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 2 covers web application security concepts. Directory enumeration is a fundamental web reconnaissance technique that reveals application structure and potentially sensitive endpoints.

content.title: Dig Deeper
content.text: What is CVE and how are vulnerabilities assigned identifiers? Research the lifecycle of a vulnerability from discovery to patch.

content.title: That’s a Reference!
content.text: CVE - Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures. MITRE Corporation. Available at: https://cve.mitre.org/about/

content.whatYoureDoing: Using nikto to scan web servers for known vulnerabilities, misconfigurations, and dangerous files.
content.whyItMatters: Nikto checks for 6,700+ dangerous files and 1,250+ outdated server versions. It quickly identifies low-hanging fruit that attackers commonly exploit.

steps.text: Run nikto against the test target:

whatThisDoes.concept: nikto is a web server vulnerability scanner that checks for thousands of known issues: outdated software, dangerous default files, misconfigurations, and insecure headers. The -h flag specifies the target host.
whatThisDoes.output: Each line prefixed with + is a finding. Look for server version disclosures, missing security headers, and OSVDB reference numbers. These findings would go into a real vulnerability assessment report.

steps.text: Watch the output — nikto reports findings as it scans
Note types of findings: server info, outdated software, dangerous files
Let it run for a few minutes

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture nikto output showing findings. Save as 11-nikto-results.png

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Wrong port: Use -p 443 for HTTPS targets — nikto defaults to port 80
Loud scanning: Nikto is NOT stealthy — every request is logged and easily detected
False positives: Verify findings manually — nikto reports potential issues, not confirmed vulnerabilities

content.title: What You Should Have
content.text: Output showing server information, potential vulnerabilities, and interesting files discovered.

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 2 discusses vulnerability scanning as part of the security assessment process. Understanding automated scanners helps both offensive and defensive security work.

content.title: Dig Deeper
content.text: How does Nikto identify vulnerabilities? Research the difference between vulnerability scanning and penetration testing.

content.title: That’s a Reference!
content.text: Nikto Documentation. CIRT, Inc. Available at: https://cirt.net/Nikto2

content.whatYoureDoing: Exploring enum4linux capabilities for SMB/Windows enumeration. Since we don’t have a live SMB target, you’ll review the tool’s help output and understand its purpose.
content.whyItMatters: SMB (Server Message Block) is ubiquitous in Windows networks. Enum4linux extracts usernames, shares, group memberships, and password policies — critical information for both attackers and security assessments. You’ll use this tool against the Brazos Financial container next week.

steps.text: Review enum4linux help:

whatThisDoes.concept: enum4linux is a tool for enumerating information from Windows/Samba systems using the SMB protocol. The -h flag displays all available options. We’re reviewing the help now because you’ll use this tool against a live target in Lab 1.3.
whatThisDoes.output: Focus on the flags listed in the Key Flags callout below. Each flag extracts a different type of information from Windows systems — usernames, file shares, password policies, and group memberships.

steps.text: Note the different enumeration options (-U for users, -S for shares, etc.)
Understand what each flag discovers

content.title: Key Flags
items: -U — Get user list
-S — Get share list
-P — Get password policy
-a — Do all simple enumeration

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture enum4linux help output. Save as 12-enum4linux-help.png

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: No SMB target: This checkpoint is documentation only — you’ll use enum4linux against real targets in Lab 1.3
Requires SMB access: Target must have ports 139/445 open for enum4linux to work

content.title: What You Should Have
content.text: Understanding of enum4linux capabilities and screenshot of help output for your runbook.

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 2 discusses network protocols and their security implications. SMB has been the source of numerous critical vulnerabilities including WannaCry and EternalBlue.

content.title: Dig Deeper
content.text: What is “null session” authentication in SMB? Why is it dangerous and how do modern Windows systems mitigate it?

content.title: That’s a Reference!
content.text: Enum4linux Manual. Portcullis Labs. Available at: https://github.com/CiscoCXSecurity/enum4linux

content.whatYoureDoing: Using searchsploit to search the Exploit Database for known vulnerabilities and exploits based on software versions discovered during reconnaissance.
content.whyItMatters: Once you know what software a target runs, you need to research whether known exploits exist. Searchsploit provides offline access to Exploit-DB, the largest public archive of exploits and vulnerable software.

steps.text: Update the database:

whatThisDoes.concept: The -u flag updates your local copy of the Exploit Database. Searchsploit works offline by searching a local mirror — updating ensures you have the latest exploits. New vulnerabilities are published daily, so keeping this current matters.

steps.text: Search for Apache vulnerabilities:

whatThisDoes.concept: This searches the local Exploit-DB for any exploit matching “apache 2.4.” In a real engagement, you’d take the exact version from your Nmap scan (e.g., Apache 2.4.7) and search for known exploits targeting it. This is where reconnaissance becomes actionable.
whatThisDoes.output: You’ll see a table of matching exploits with titles, types (local, remote, webapps, DoS), and file paths. The path column tells you where the exploit code lives on your system if you need to examine it.

steps.text: Note the exploit titles, types, and paths
Try searching for OpenSSH:

whatThisDoes.output: Compare the results to your Apache search. Notice the different exploit types and how the version numbers in the titles help you narrow down which exploits apply to a specific target. In practice, you’d cross-reference these results with the exact version string from your Nmap scan.

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture searchsploit results for Apache. Save as 13-searchsploit-apache.png

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Too broad: “apache” returns thousands — add version numbers to narrow results
Outdated database: Run searchsploit -u periodically to update
Not all exploits work: Many require specific conditions — read the exploit code before attempting

content.title: What You Should Have
content.text: Search results showing exploit titles, paths, and platforms for Apache and OpenSSH.

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 2 discusses vulnerability research and the importance of staying current with security advisories. Exploit databases are essential tools for both attackers and defenders.

content.title: Dig Deeper
content.text: What’s the difference between a CVE and an exploit? Research how vulnerabilities are disclosed and how long it typically takes for exploits to appear.

content.title: That’s a Reference!
content.text: Exploit Database. Offensive Security. Available at: https://www.exploit-db.com/

items.label: Reconnaissance is the Foundation
items.text: Every engagement starts with information gathering. nmap gives you open ports and versions; netcat confirms banners manually; dirb finds hidden paths; nikto flags known misconfigurations. Together, these six tools build a complete picture of a target’s attack surface.

items.label: Version Intelligence Drives Exploitation
items.text: A version string from nmap or netcat banner grabbing feeds directly into searchsploit. “Apache 2.4.7” becomes a CVE list. This chain — discover, identify, research — is the core offensive workflow.

items.label: SMB Is a Username Factory
items.text: enum4linux null session enumeration extracts usernames, shares, and password policies without authenticating. That user list becomes your credential attack target.

items.label: Your Logs Record This Reconnaissance
items.text: Every nmap scan, dirb request, and nikto probe leaves traces in access logs. Defenders who run these tools against their own infrastructure know exactly what a scan looks like — and can build detection rules accordingly.

items.label: Version Banners Are Information Leaks
items.text: Service banners reveal software versions to anyone who connects. Suppressing banners and obscuring version strings reduces the reconnaissance value an attacker gains from a scan.

items.label: Authorized Scanning Is Blue Team Work
items.text: Running these tools on your own infrastructure before an attacker does is called attack surface management. Everything you discover today, a defender should already know about their own environment.

purpleTeam.connection: The six tools in this lab are used identically by both sides. Red team runs them offensively to map targets. Blue team runs them defensively to audit their own exposure. The difference is authorization and intent — not the tools themselves.

10-dirb-results.png — dirb discovered directories
11-nikto-results.png — nikto vulnerability scan output
12-enum4linux-help.png — enum4linux help output
13-searchsploit-apache.png — searchsploit Apache results

needHelp.lateWorkPolicy: Late work is not accepted. Drop policies exist for emergencies.

permitted: Explain command syntax and tool functionality
prohibited: Complete hands-on exercises on your behalf
genAI.principle: AI is a tutor, not a ghostwriter.
